title: Biceps Brachii
text: The biceps brachii arises from two heads: the long head from the supraglenoid tubercle of the scapula, and the short head from the coracoid process. Both heads converge into a single muscle belly that inserts via a tendon into the radial tuberosity of the radius. The bicipital aponeurosis extends medially from the tendon to the forearm fascia. The muscle is innervated by the musculocutaneous nerve (C5–C6) and acts primarily as a forearm flexor and supinator.

title: Triceps Brachii
text: The triceps brachii is the sole extensor of the forearm and arises from three heads. The long head originates at the infraglenoid tubercle of the scapula; the lateral and medial heads arise from the posterior surface of the humerus. All three heads converge into a broad tendon that inserts into the olecranon of the ulna. The primary nerve supply is the radial nerve (C6–C8), which courses through the radial groove on the posterior humerus.

title: Median Nerve in the Forearm
text: The median nerve descends between the two heads of the pronator teres before entering the forearm proper. It passes deep to the flexor digitorum superficialis, which serves as its major muscular landmark, and gives a named branch—the anterior interosseous nerve—that supplies the deep flexors of the index and middle fingers. The median nerve then travels through the carpal tunnel beneath the flexor retinaculum to enter the hand, where it provides sensory coverage of the lateral three-and-a-half digits.

title: Ulnar Nerve
text: The ulnar nerve passes posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus, where it is palpable in the cubital tunnel. Within the forearm it innervates flexor carpi ulnaris and the medial half of flexor digitorum profundus. The nerve then enters the hand via Guyon's canal at the wrist, lateral to the pisiform bone. Compression within this canal or at the elbow produces characteristic weakness of the intrinsic hand muscles and sensory loss over the medial one-and-a-half digits.

title: Blood Supply of the Femoral Head
text: The femoral head depends on three vascular sources for its blood supply. The most critical is the medial circumflex femoral artery, which gives rise to the retinacular vessels that ascend along the femoral neck within the capsule and supply the majority of the femoral head. A minor contribution comes through the ligamentum teres, which carries the acetabular branch of the obturator artery. Disruption of the retinacular vessels, as occurs in displaced femoral neck fractures, is the principal cause of avascular necrosis.

title: Hip Joint Capsule and Ligaments
text: The fibrous capsule of the hip joint is reinforced by three thickened bands: the iliofemoral ligament anteriorly, the pubofemoral ligament inferiorly, and the ischiofemoral ligament posteriorly. The iliofemoral ligament is the strongest ligament in the body and prevents hyperextension during standing. Deepening the socket, the acetabular labrum is a fibrocartilaginous rim attached to the margin of the acetabulum; it increases joint stability and contact area, and its tears are a common source of hip pain in young adults.

title: Brachial Plexus
text: The brachial plexus is formed from the anterior rami of C5–T1 and is classically described in five successive segments. The roots combine into three trunks in the posterior triangle of the neck: superior, middle, and inferior. Each trunk splits into anterior and posterior divisions, which regroup into three cords in the axilla. From the cords arise the five terminal branches: musculocutaneous, median, ulnar, radial, and axillary nerves. These nerves distribute motor and sensory supply throughout the entire upper limb.

title: Superior Mediastinum
text: The superior mediastinum extends from the thoracic inlet to the sternal angle of Louis. Its major arterial landmark is the aortic arch, from which the brachiocephalic, left common carotid, and left subclavian arteries arise. Flanking the arteries, the brachiocephalic veins drain venous blood from the head, neck, and upper limbs and unite to form the superior vena cava. The trachea descends centrally, bifurcating at the carina below. The thymus occupies the anterior superior mediastinum during childhood and is progressively replaced by fatty tissue with age.

title: Inferior Mediastinum
text: The inferior mediastinum lies below the sternal angle and is subdivided into anterior, middle, and posterior compartments. The middle compartment contains the pericardium surrounding the heart and the roots of the great vessels. The posterior compartment houses the esophagus, closely related to the descending thoracic aorta running along its left side. The thoracic duct, azygos system, and sympathetic chains also traverse this region. Surgical access to the esophagus is most commonly achieved via a right thoracotomy to avoid the descending thoracic aorta.

title: Valves of the Heart
text: The heart contains four valves that maintain unidirectional blood flow. On the right side, the tricuspid valve guards the atrioventricular orifice between the right atrium and right ventricle; the pulmonary valve controls outflow to the pulmonary trunk. On the left side, the mitral valve lies between the left atrium and left ventricle; the aortic valve sits at the base of the ascending aorta. All four valves are anchored to the fibrous skeleton of the heart and supported by chordae tendineae and papillary muscles where applicable.

title: Lobes of the Liver
text: The liver is conventionally divided into four anatomical lobes visible on its visceral surface. The right lobe is the largest and lies to the right of the gallbladder fossa. The left lobe extends across the midline. Separating these two lobes anteriorly is the falciform ligament, which carries the ligamentum teres from the umbilicus. Medial subdivisions of the right lobe include the caudate lobe superiorly and the quadrate lobe inferiorly, both separated from each other by the porta hepatis and its enclosed neurovascular structures.

title: The Nephron
text: The nephron is the structural and functional unit of the kidney, with approximately one million per kidney. Each nephron begins at the glomerulus, a tuft of capillaries enclosed by the Bowman capsule, where hydrostatic pressure drives filtration of plasma into the tubular system. The filtrate then passes through the proximal convoluted tubule, the loop of Henle, and the distal convoluted tubule, during which water, ions, and organic solutes are selectively reabsorbed or secreted before the resulting urine drains into the collecting duct.

title: Bronchial Tree
text: The trachea divides at the carina into the right and left main bronchus. The right main bronchus is shorter, wider, and more vertical, making it the preferential site for aspirated foreign bodies. Each main bronchus subdivides into lobar bronchi: three on the right corresponding to the upper, middle, and lower lobes, and two on the left. Each lobar bronchus then divides into segmental bronchi, one per bronchopulmonary segment. The bronchial tree continues branching through smaller bronchioles until reaching the terminal respiratory units.

title: Foramina of the Skull Base
text: The skull base contains numerous foramina through which cranial nerves and vessels pass. The largest opening, the foramen magnum, transmits the brainstem, vertebral arteries, and accessory nerve roots. The optic canal in the lesser wing of the sphenoid carries the optic nerve and ophthalmic artery. The foramen ovale in the greater sphenoid wing transmits the mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve. The jugular foramen, at the junction of the occipital and temporal bones, conveys cranial nerves IX, X, and XI along with the internal jugular vein.

title: Ligaments of the Knee
text: The knee joint relies on four principal ligaments and two fibrocartilaginous menisci for stability. The anterior cruciate ligament prevents anterior tibial translation and is one of the most commonly injured ligaments in sport. The posterior cruciate ligament resists posterior tibial displacement. On the medial side, the medial collateral ligament guards against valgus stress and is frequently injured alongside the anterior cruciate ligament. The menisci are C-shaped fibrocartilage structures wedged between the femoral condyles and the tibial plateau, distributing load and deepening joint congruity.

title: The Inguinal Canal
text: The inguinal canal is an oblique passage through the lower anterior abdominal wall, approximately 4 cm long. It begins at the deep inguinal ring, a defect in the transversalis fascia lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels, and ends at the superficial inguinal ring, an opening in the external oblique aponeurosis above the pubic tubercle. In males the canal transmits the spermatic cord; in females it conveys the round ligament of the uterus. The roof is formed by arching fibres of the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles.
